questions.question: What is Jetpack Compose, and how does it differ from the traditional Android View system?
questions.title: What is Jetpack Compose, and how does it differ from the traditional Android View system?

sections.heading: Jetpack Compose
sections.content: Jetpack Compose is Android's modern declarative UI toolkit. Differences from traditional Views:

sections.heading: Jetpack Compose
items: Declarative vs Imperative: Compose describes what UI should look like.
Less boilerplate: No XML layouts.
Reactive: UI updates automatically when state changes.
Composables: UI elements are Kotlin functions.
Works seamlessly with existing Android components.

questions.question: Explain the concept of declarative UI in Jetpack Compose.
questions.title: Explain the concept of declarative UI in Jetpack Compose.

sections.heading: Declarative UI
sections.content: In declarative UI, you describe the UI for a given state instead of specifying steps to update it.

sections.heading: Declarative UI
items: Compose automatically updates UI on state changes.

questions.question: Declarative UI vs Imperative UI
questions.title: Declarative UI vs Imperative UI

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: Imperative UI:

sections.heading: Introduction
items: Describes how to update UI step-by-step
Used in XML + View system
Requires manual UI updates

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: Declarative UI:

sections.heading: Introduction
items: Describes what UI should display based on state
Used in Jetpack Compose
UI updates automatically

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: In Short:

sections.heading: Introduction
items: Imperative = Manual updates
Declarative = State-driven UI

questions.question: What are Composable functions?
questions.title: What are Composable functions?

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: Composable functions are the basic building blocks of UI in Jetpack Compose. They are functions marked with the @Composable annotation that define how the UI should look.

sections.heading: Key Features
items: Used to create UI components
Can be combined (composed) to build complex layouts
Automatically update UI when state changes
Written using Kotlin functions instead of XML

questions.question: What are the benefits of Jetpack Compose?
questions.title: What are the benefits of Jetpack Compose?

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: Jetpack Compose is Android's modern UI toolkit that lets developers build UI using Kotlin instead of XML, making development faster and simpler.

sections.heading: Introduction
items: Less Code – Removes XML layouts and reduces boilerplate.
Faster Development – Declarative UI with live previews speeds up development.
Easy Theming – Built-in Material Design and dark mode support.
Reusable UI – Composable functions improve modularity and maintainability.
Better State Management – UI updates automatically when data changes.
Improved Performance – Smart recomposition updates only necessary UI parts.
Interoperability – Works with existing XML Views for gradual migration.

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: Conclusion: Compose simplifies Android UI development with cleaner code, better performance, and faster UI creation.

questions.question: Jetpack Compose vs Android View System
questions.title: Jetpack Compose vs Android View System

sections.heading: Introduction
items: Feature: UI Approach | Compose: Declarative | View System: Imperative
Feature: Layout | Compose: Composables | View System: XML + Views
Feature: State Handling | Compose: Automatic recomposition | View System: Manual updates
Feature: Boilerplate | Compose: Less | View System: More
Feature: Interoperability | Compose: Works with Views | View System: Views can host Compose via AndroidView

questions.question: Explain the role of @Composable in Jetpack Compose.
questions.title: Explain the role of @Composable in Jetpack Compose.

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: @Composable turns a Kotlin function into a UI function that builds and updates UI in Jetpack Compose.

sections.heading: Defines UI Components
sections.content: Used to create UI elements like text, buttons, layouts, or screens.

sections.heading: Enables Declarative UI
sections.content: Describes what UI should look like. UI updates automatically when data changes.

sections.heading: Supports Recomposition
sections.content: Only affected composables are redrawn when state changes, improving performance.

sections.heading: Allows UI Composition
sections.content: Composable functions can call other composables to build reusable and complex UI.

questions.question: Explain the lifecycle of a Composable.
questions.title: Explain the lifecycle of a Composable.

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: The lifecycle of a composable defines how it is created, updated, and removed from the UI in Jetpack Compose.

sections.heading: 🆕 Composition
sections.content: When a composable is first called, Compose creates the UI and adds it to the screen.

sections.heading: Recomposition
sections.content: When the state used inside a composable changes, Compose re-executes the function and updates only the required UI parts.

sections.heading: Disposal
sections.content: When a composable is no longer needed, it is removed from the UI and its resources are cleared.

sections.heading: State Awareness
sections.content: Compose tracks state changes automatically, ensuring the UI stays updated and efficient.

questions.question: How do you handle lifecycle events in Compose functions?
questions.title: How do you handle lifecycle events in Compose functions?

sections.heading: Lifecycle Handling
items: LaunchedEffect → Run suspend code when key changes
DisposableEffect → Cleanup resources
SideEffect → Run after successful composition
Observe LifecycleOwner using LocalLifecycleOwner.current

sections.heading: Lifecycle Handling

questions.question: What is State in Compose?
questions.title: What is State in Compose?

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: State in Jetpack Compose represents data that can change and affect the UI. When state changes, Compose automatically updates the UI.

sections.heading: Holds UI Data
sections.content: Stores values like text input, selection, or visibility.

sections.heading: Triggers UI Updates
sections.content: State changes automatically recompose and update the UI.

sections.heading: Managed with State APIs
sections.content: Handled using tools like remember and mutableStateOf.

sections.heading: Enables Reactive UI
sections.content: Helps create dynamic UI that responds to user actions and data changes.

questions.question: How does state management work in Jetpack Compose, and what are the common state types?
questions.title: How does state management work in Jetpack Compose, and what are the common state types?

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: State management in Jetpack Compose controls how UI data is stored, updated, and observed. When state changes, Compose automatically recomposes and updates the UI.

sections.heading: How State Management Works
sections.content: Compose observes state values used inside composable functions. When the state changes, only the affected UI is recomposed.

sections.heading: remember
sections.content: Stores state inside a composable and survives recomposition.

sections.heading: mutableStateOf
sections.content: Creates observable state that triggers UI updates when changed.

sections.heading: rememberSaveable
sections.content: Saves state across configuration changes like screen rotation.

sections.heading: State / MutableState
sections.content: Wrapper classes that hold and observe state values.

questions.question: What is MutableState and how does recomposition happen?
questions.title: What is MutableState and how does recomposition happen?

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: MutableState is a state holder in Jetpack Compose that stores a value and notifies Compose when the value changes.

sections.heading: What is MutableState?
sections.content: A wrapper around a value that can be observed and updated, usually created using mutableStateOf.

sections.heading: How Recomposition Happens
sections.content: When the state value changes, Compose re-executes composables using that state and updates only the affected UI.

sections.heading: Benefit
sections.content: Helps create reactive UI with automatic and efficient updates.

questions.question: Stateful composable vs Stateless composable
questions.title: Stateful composable vs Stateless composable

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: In Jetpack Compose, composables can be stateful or stateless depending on whether they hold or rely on state.

sections.heading: Stateful Composable
items: Holds its own state using remember or mutableStateOf.
Responsible for updating itself when the state changes.
Example: A text field that manages its input internally.

sections.heading: Stateless Composable
items: Does not hold state internally.
Receives state and events via parameters.
Relies on parent composables to manage state.
Example: A button that displays a value passed from its parent.

questions.question: Difference between Stateless and Stateful composables
questions.title: Difference between Stateless and Stateful composables

sections.heading: Stateful Composable
items: Holds and manages its own state.
Updates itself when the state changes.
Less reusable due to internal state.
Example: A TextField managing its input internally.

sections.heading: Stateless Composable
items: Does not hold state; receives state from parent.
UI updates rely on external state changes.
Highly reusable and easier to test.
Example: A Button displaying text passed from parent.

questions.question: What is State Hoisting?
questions.title: What is State Hoisting?

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: State Hoisting in Jetpack Compose is moving state up to a parent composable, making the child stateless and reusable.

sections.heading: How It Works
items: Parent holds the state and passes it to the child.
Child emits events to update the parent's state.

sections.heading: Benefits
items: Makes UI components reusable and testable.
Separates state management from UI.
Enables unidirectional data flow.

sections.heading: Example

questions.question: How to retain state across recomposition and configuration changes?
questions.title: How to retain state across recomposition and configuration changes?

sections.heading: Across Recomposition
items: Use remember to keep state during recomposition.

sections.heading: Across Configuration Changes
items: Use rememberSaveable to retain state during recomposition and rotations.

sections.heading: Across Configuration Changes
sections.content: Key: remember → recomposition only, rememberSaveable → recomposition + config changes.

questions.question: What is Recomposition?
questions.title: What is Recomposition?

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: Recomposition in Jetpack Compose is the process where a composable function is re-executed to update the UI when the state it depends on changes.

sections.heading: How It Works
items: Compose tracks state used in composables.
When the state changes, only the affected composables are recomposed.
Unaffected parts of the UI are not redrawn, improving performance.

sections.heading: Benefits
items: Ensures UI is always in sync with data.
Provides efficient and reactive updates without manual UI refresh.

questions.question: How to avoid recomposition of a composable if the state is not changed? (Smart Recomposition)
questions.title: How to avoid recomposition of a composable if the state is not changed? (Smart Recomposition)

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: To prevent recomposition when state hasn't changed:

sections.heading: Introduction
items: remember – Keeps state across recompositions.

sections.heading: Introduction
items: key – Recompose only when the key changes.

sections.heading: Introduction
items: derivedStateOf – Recompose only if the derived value changes.

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.code: val derivedValue by remember { derivedStateOf { state1 + state2 } }

sections.heading: Introduction
items: Immutable/Stable Data – Helps Compose skip recomposition if the value is unchanged.

questions.question: Does re-composition of ComposeItem1 affect ComposeItem2? If yes, how?
questions.title: Does re-composition of ComposeItem1 affect ComposeItem2? If yes, how?

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: No, recomposition in Jetpack Compose is localized.

sections.heading: How It Works
items: Compose tracks which state each composable uses.
If ComposeItem1's state changes, only ComposeItem1 is recomposed.
ComposeItem2 remains unaffected if it doesn't depend on the same state.

sections.heading: Key Point
items: Only composables that observe changed state are recomposed.
This makes Compose efficient by avoiding unnecessary UI redraws.

questions.question: What are stable types that can skip recomposition?
questions.title: What are stable types that can skip recomposition?

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: Stable types are objects or values that don't change unexpectedly, allowing Compose to skip recomposition when they remain the same.

sections.heading: Primitive types**: **Int**, **Float**, **Boolean**, **String
items: Immutable collections: List, Map, Set from Kotlin's immutable types

sections.heading: Objects marked with **@Stable**
items: Functions / Lambdas that don't capture changing state

sections.heading: Key Point
items: If a composable uses only stable types, Compose can avoid recomposing it when unrelated state changes, improving performance.

questions.question: What are side effects in Compose?
questions.title: What are side effects in Compose?

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: Side effects in Compose are operations that interact with the outside world or cause changes outside the composable scope, such as updating state, launching coroutines, or performing I/O.

sections.heading: Examples of Side Effects
items: Showing a Toast or Snackbar
Navigating to another screen
Writing to a database or shared preferences
Launching coroutines for API calls

sections.heading: Handling Side Effects
sections.content: Compose provides special APIs to safely handle side effects:

sections.heading: Handling Side Effects
items: LaunchedEffect – Runs a coroutine tied to the composable lifecycle
SideEffect – Runs after every successful recomposition
DisposableEffect – Cleans up resources when the composable leaves composition

questions.question: Difference between LaunchedEffect and DisposableEffect
questions.title: Difference between LaunchedEffect and DisposableEffect

sections.heading: LaunchedEffect
items: Runs a coroutine tied to composable lifecycle.
Used for async tasks (API calls, animations).
Cancels automatically when key changes or composable leaves.

sections.heading: DisposableEffect
items: Runs side effects with cleanup when entering/leaving composition.
Used for resources or listeners.

questions.question: What is rememberCoroutineScope and its use cases?
questions.title: What is rememberCoroutineScope and its use cases?

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: rememberCoroutineScope is a Compose API that provides a lifecycle-aware CoroutineScope tied to a composable. It allows launching coroutines from UI events like clicks or gestures.

sections.heading: How It Works
items: Returns a CoroutineScope that cancels automatically when the composable leaves the composition.
Unlike LaunchedEffect, it can be used inside event handlers (e.g., button clicks).

sections.heading: Use Cases
items: Performing asynchronous tasks on user interaction

sections.heading: Use Cases
Use Cases
items: Animations triggered by events
Temporary background work tied to a composable

questions.question: How to launch a coroutine from a composable function (LaunchedEffect)?
questions.title: How to launch a coroutine from a composable function (LaunchedEffect)?

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: LaunchedEffect lets you launch a coroutine tied to a composable's lifecycle. It cancels automatically when the key changes or the composable leaves.

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: Use Case: Initial data loading, animations, or side effects in composables.

questions.question: How to launch a coroutine from a non-composable function but tied to composition (rememberCoroutineScope)?
questions.title: How to launch a coroutine from a non-composable function but tied to composition (rememberCoroutineScope)?

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: rememberCoroutineScope provides a lifecycle-aware CoroutineScope that can be used in event handlers or non-composable functions inside a composable.

sections.heading: How It Works
items: The scope is tied to the composable's lifecycle.
Cancels automatically when the composable leaves composition.
Useful for launching coroutines imperatively (e.g., on button click).

sections.heading: Example
